items.title: Sweep one complexity axis at a time
items.avoid: Changing depth, learning rate, and feature set simultaneously so you cannot tell which knob moved validation error.
items.prefer: Fix other settings; plot train vs CV error over one hyperparameter (e.g. max_depth 1–20).
items.why: Isolated sweeps reveal the U-curve and the validation minimum clearly.
items.example: Hold min_samples_leaf=5; only vary max_depth when comparing tree complexity.

items.title: Use cross-validation for complexity selection
items.avoid: Trusting a single train/val split when choosing degree or depth — lucky splits favor too-complex models.
items.prefer: 5-fold CV mean validation error at each complexity level; pick simplest in the best band.
items.why: CV averages variance across folds — more reliable sweet-spot estimate on small and medium data.

items.title: Prefer simplest model near the validation minimum
items.avoid: Always selecting the absolute lowest validation score if a much simpler model is within 1–2% error.
items.prefer: Define a tolerance band around min val error; choose lowest complexity inside the band.
items.why: Reduces variance, training time, and deployment risk with negligible metric loss.
items.example: Depth 6 val AUC 0.872 vs depth 10 at 0.875 — pick depth 6 for production.

items.title: Match complexity to available data
items.avoid: Deploying high-capacity models (deep nets, k=1, degree ≥ 10) on hundreds of labeled rows.
items.prefer: Start with baselines appropriate to n; increase capacity only when CV shows underfitting.
items.why: Small n cannot support high variance models — validation curves peak early on the complexity axis.
items.example: 500-image medical set: small CNN or transfer learning with frozen backbone before full fine-tune.
